comment_text.sn22.51:1.4: “Relishing” (nandi) and “greed” (or “lust”, rāga) are similar in meaning, but with distinct stresses: “relishing” enjoys the object of desire, while “greed” is inflamed by passion for it.

root_text.sn22.51:1.2: “Aniccaññeva, bhikkhave, bhikkhu rūpaṁ aniccanti passati. Sāssa hoti sammādiṭṭhi.
root_text.sn22.51:1.6: Aniccaññeva, bhikkhave, bhikkhu vedanaṁ aniccanti passati. Sāssa hoti sammādiṭṭhi.
root_text.sn22.51:1.10: Aniccaññeva, bhikkhave, bhikkhu saññaṁ aniccanti passati …pe…
root_text.sn22.51:1.11: anicceyeva bhikkhave, bhikkhu saṅkhāre aniccāti passati. Sāssa hoti sammādiṭṭhi.
root_text.sn22.51:1.15: Aniccaññeva, bhikkhave, bhikkhu viññāṇaṁ aniccanti passati. Sāssa hoti sammādiṭṭhi.

translation_text.sn22.51:1.2: “Mendicants, form really is impermanent. A mendicant sees that it is impermanent: that’s their right view.
translation_text.sn22.51:1.4: When relishing ends, greed ends. When greed ends, relishing ends.
translation_text.sn22.51:1.5: When relishing and greed end, the mind is freed, and is said to be well freed.
translation_text.sn22.51:1.15: Consciousness really is impermanent. A mendicant sees that it is impermanent: that’s their right view.
translation_text.sn22.51:1.17: When relishing ends, greed ends. When greed ends, relishing ends.
translation_text.sn22.51:1.18: When relishing and greed end, the mind is freed, and is said to be well freed.”
